Mariano delivered our MVP in record time with exceptional quality. His technical expertise and communication made the entire process smooth.
Migrated a Next.js portfolio site to Astro 5 for better performance and developer experience.
The existing Next.js site had grown complex with unnecessary client-side JS, slow build times, and a bloated bundle for what was essentially a static marketing/portfolio site.

Migrated to Astro 5 with islands architecture, keeping React only where interactivity is needed. Set up content collections for blog posts and work case studies, added i18n routing, and implemented Tailwind v4.
90+ Lighthouse scores across all metrics. Build time reduced by 60%. Zero JS shipped by default for static pages.
This case study documents the migration process from Next.js to Astro 5.
A sample blog post demonstrating MDX with React components.

import Counter from "../../components/Counter";
import AlertBox from "../../components/AlertBox";

Welcome to the blog! This post is written in **MDX**, so you can use React components inline.

<AlertBox type="success" title="Great News!" client:load>
  React components now work seamlessly in MDX files!
</AlertBox>

## Interactive Counter

Here is an interactive counter component:

<Counter client:load />

## Alert Examples

<AlertBox type="info" title="Information" client:load>
  This is an informational alert.
</AlertBox>

<AlertBox type="warning" client:load>
  This is a warning without a title.
</AlertBox>

<AlertBox type="error" title="Error" client:load>
  Something went wrong!
</AlertBox>

## How It Works

1. Import your React component at the top of the MDX file
2. Use it with the `client:load` directive
3. Pass props as normal React props
